title: Platonic: How the Science of Attachment Can Help You Make—and Keep—Friends
author: Marisa G. Franco

chapters.title: The highest joy
chapters.text:
Aristotle, one of history's greatest philosophers, once wrote, "No one would choose to live without friends even if he had all the other goods." His student Plato later expanded on this wisdom, developing ideas about friendship so profound that centuries later, "platonic friendship" became a term for deep, non-romantic bonds outside the family.

Unfortunately, modern society often views friendships as pleasant but secondary to romantic love. This perspective directly contradicts Plato's belief that friendship represents something purer and more virtuous precisely because it isn't driven by sexual attraction or impulses. He saw this kind of relationship as a rare kinship of souls, which is perhaps the most generous gift fate can offer.

The opinions of others may be necessary, but your inner voice should always be the deciding factor.

Although over two millennia have passed, "platonic relationships" still remain necessary for human well-being. Marisa G. Franco has found that a wide range of social contacts reduces the risk of death by 45%, not to mention improving quality of life. Yet, many adults feel lonely and don't know how to make new friends or what to do when old friendships begin to bring more pain than joy.

If you feel the same way, don't be discouraged. This guide will give you practical tips on how to make and keep friends because you deserve to have people who appreciate you at your side.

chapters.title: The language of affection
chapters.text:
Imagine yourself at a picnic in the park. The sun is shining, the birds are singing, and you are sitting under a tree, watching the people around you. Some play board games, others take photos, some laugh, others quarrel, and a few quietly lie beside each other reading.

You can observe these different dynamics because everyone forms friendships in their own way. But why is that so? What exactly dictates these personal approaches to friendship? The answer is simple: the past.

A person's character and attitude toward relationships form from the day they are born. Like a sponge, a child absorbs the behavior of others and begins to show attachment to others in one of three ways based on these experiences:

• Secure attachment: This style develops when people close to the child show consistent love and support regardless of circumstances. In this case, the person is not afraid to open up to others, accept affection, and understand both their own needs and others' boundaries.
• Anxious attachment: People with this type subconsciously expect others to abandon them, so they often sacrifice themselves in relationships and allow others to violate their boundaries. They typically give more than they receive and live in constant fear of disappointing their partners.
• Avoidant attachment: Instead of satisfying others, those with this attachment build a wall between themselves and the world. They are afraid to accept attention, and they don't let anyone get close because they are scared to get hurt.

If you recognize anxious or avoidant patterns in yourself, relax. Little by little, you can gradually develop a more secure one, although this process is long and requires effort.

The past influences the present but does not determine it.

Healing comes through corrective experiences with others. When you have good friends, note the moments when your expectations weren't met. Do this consistently, and you'll build new connections that will help you feel safer in your friendships and interactions with people. You'll also learn more about their behavioral patterns.

chapters.title: Friendship takes effort
chapters.text:
To show affection, you need people around you who also want the same thing. Even so, many adults struggle with finding dear ones to care about. In childhood, we could simply walk up to anyone on the playground and offer to be friends, but in adulthood, this method no longer works, and the search for a soul mate evolves into a confusing quest.

Life offers everyone a chance, but you must dare to take yours.

The fact is that adult friendships don't simply happen; they require consistent effort and often personal initiative. This reality might feel frustrating, but it also means that as adults, we can choose the best people for our environment. Accept the element of surprise in your life to take the first step in this direction.

Every day presents at least one opportunity to form new acquaintances, some of which may develop into meaningful friendships. You don't need to be an extrovert to do this; you simply need to allow yourself to open up a little.

Is it difficult? Often, people struggle at this stage because they think people don't like them and will use their feelings against them. While this can happen, it's just as rare as someone showing unexpected affection. Most people respond neutrally to new contacts, so if you feel that someone doesn't like you during an interaction, try analyzing why. Do their words, gestures, and facial expressions tell you that? If so, you'll understand them better, and if not, remind yourself that the brain can be deceiving.

With this mindset, move on to action. Here's one of the easiest ways to make a friend:

• Find and attend a group activity that genuinely interests you.
• Remind yourself that the people in this group like you.
• Overcome avoidance by talking to different group members.
• Ask your favorite person in the group to spend time together.

Regular participation will ensure that you subconsciously matter more to the group members and that they mean more to you. In addition, you can get to know them organically and better step by step to choose wisely.

chapters.title: Vulnerability makes you strong
chapters.text:
Let's imagine that you've found a friend. What happens next? Many people cannot answer this question because, even after a successful meeting, they can only get so close before hitting an emotional wall. The reason for this is the fear of vulnerability.

Even the best warriors remove their armor when the battle ends.

The anxiety about being vulnerable is widespread in the modern world, as society celebrates pain tolerance, resilience, and strength. Yet few realize that vulnerability doesn't contradict these concepts but is a part of them. After all, it takes a lot of courage to bear your heart.

Doubts about this bold action are understandable. Enemies might exploit such moments to hurt you, so you shouldn't share your deepest feelings with just anyone. But friends will behave quite differently. By showing them your weaknesses, you invite their support and deepen your bond in a shame-free environment. Moreover, they may start thinking of you as sincere and authentic.

Being invulnerable doesn't eliminate your weaknesses; it just keeps you from showing strength alongside it. ~ Marisa G. Franco, PhD

Still finding it hard to take that first step? Don't worry, it's okay. Being vulnerable is not easy, but practicing self-compassion can help you move forward. It consists of three steps:

• Self-kindness: Offering yourself the same understanding and compassion you'd give to a friend. When you fail at something, don't let your inner critic cloud your thoughts. There are always reasons.
• Mindfulness: Forming balanced responses to unpleasant thoughts and feelings without suppressing them or letting them consume you. When facing disappointment, don't beat yourself over it, but don't ignore the sadness of failure either.
• Common humanity: Recognizing that your experiences connect you to others rather than isolate you. Remember that your struggles and mistakes are part of being human because everyone messes up sometimes.

Being in tune with your feelings makes it easier to express them to others. You can only share what's in your heart if you recognize it yourself. But how do you put this into practice in your everyday friendships? Keep going — we've got a set of practical strategies waiting for you.

chapters.title: Be loved as you are
chapters.text:
Vulnerability always goes hand in hand with authenticity, which is the state of presence a person can access when they don't feel threatened. It's who we are underneath our armor and all the security mechanisms. This true personality is what our friends see, and it's what nurtures friendships because humans are social creatures and subconsciously seek to show empathy and compassion for others.

Be authentic: people are looking for friends for themselves, not for one of their masks.

Being authentic is easy when things are going well, but what do you do in an unpleasant situation? First, pay attention to your defense mechanisms.

Look at what you naturally do when feeling threatened and think about these patterns and their triggers. Pause and use your breath to calm yourself. Switch your focus from protection to openness. You can't avoid stress altogether, but by using this technique, you can embrace your higher self even during chaotic moments.

If you can't be authentic, not due to the specific danger but because of social anxiety, try redirecting your focus from your perceived flaws to the people you're interacting with. They're just like you, imperfect, and probably feel the same way.

If someone wants to be your friend, they will accept your authenticity and respect your values, even when your perspectives differ. If they don't, are they worth worrying about?

Friendships between people from disadvantaged and advantaged groups can often be complicated because their backgrounds are so different. If you are more fortunate than your friend, try to offset the power dynamics by actively trying to understand their perspective. Instead of advocating or accusing, ask thoughtful questions to understand their position better.

If you're from a disadvantaged group, follow the three Vs rule:

• Vet friends and befriend only those who believe in your values.
• Be vulnerable about your feelings for your identity group.
• Voice any concerns freely.

Remember that no matter the circumstances, friendship is always built on trust. No matter how insecure you might feel, there are people ready to love you as you are.

chapters.title: Conflict is not the end
chapters.text:
Sometimes, even the best friendships have conflicts. Though painful and disturbing, these moments aren't disasters — they're growth opportunities that can bring you closer. Ignoring problems is much worse because hidden issues will accumulate and eventually poison the relationship.

Challenges are inevitable — it's how you respond that determines the outcome.

First, decide whether you will invest energy in resolving the conflict. Weigh the pros and cons of the friendship and determine if it's generally positive or negative in your life. Don't feel guilty if you don't see the point in continuing. People sometimes grow apart from those once close to them. It's a natural part of life, and you should view such connections not as losses but as valuable experiences.

If you think the friendship is still worth the effort, there's a step-by-step method for managing conflict effectively:

• Soothe: Take time to process your reactions.
• Express: Communicate how much you value the friendship and highlight how it leads to your commitment to working through issues.
• Share your emotions, but don't blame your friend.
• Ask for your friend's perspective: If needed, soothe your triggers by breathing and de-escalating by gently admitting faults, asking additional questions, or taking a break.
• Respond to your friend's concerns with understanding, validation, and care. For example, repeat what they've said, say it's understandable, and share your will to improve.

To get through a conflict better, separate it from your self-esteem. Even the closest friends fight sometimes, and these disagreements don't affect your value to the world. If the argument ends badly, consider revisiting the issue when emotions have settled.

If the conflict reflects more unhealthy dynamics, think about cutting ties. If your friendship is excellent, beyond the immediate problem, think about the level to which you want to integrate your friend into your life.

Remember that even if the conflict goes wrong, you do right by acting with integrity. ~ Marisa G. Franco, PhD

Did you know? Nicola Tesla and Mark Twain were close friends. Tesla once had Twain stand on an electrical oscillator, claiming it would improve his health. Instead, it worked as a laxative, sending Twain rushing to the restroom.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Friendships aren't just coincidences. They develop through intentional actions, vulnerability, and effort. Here's how you can nurture meaningful, lasting connections starting today:

• Audit your friendship circle: List your closest five friends and reflect on how they make you feel. Do they uplift you, challenge you, or drain you? Identify any imbalances and decide where you want to invest more of yourself.
• Initiate one small act of friendship this week: Whether it's sending a quick check-in message, sharing a memory, or extending an invite to catch up, take a small but meaningful step toward maintaining your bonds.
• Practice "micro-moments" of connection: In your daily interactions, make eye contact, use your friend's name, and ask specific follow-up questions about their life. These small gestures build trust over time.
• Use the "Three-Day Rule" for conflicts: When disagreements arise, give yourself three days before making any decisions about the friendship. This approach prevents impulsive reactions and allows time for reflection and perspective.

Read it till the end?
